title_en: Iman, Islam, and Ihsan: The Hierarchy of the Deen
content_en:
The Salafi creed includes a precise and carefully nuanced understanding of iman (faith), Islam (submission), and ihsan (excellence in worship), drawn from the famous hadith of Jibril in which the angel came to the Prophet in the form of a man and asked him about these three dimensions of religion. This chapter examines how the Salaf understood these concepts and how their understanding differed from those of deviant theological schools.

Iman in the Salafi understanding is defined as: affirmation with the tongue (iqrar bil-lisan), conviction with the heart (tasdiq bil-qalb), and action with the limbs (amal bil-jawarih). This comprehensive definition means that iman is not merely an internal state of heart but one that produces both verbal profession and righteous deeds. It also means that iman is not static — it increases with obedience and decreases with sin, as affirmed by numerous Companions and early scholars. The Salaf cited Quranic evidence for this: 'That they may increase in iman together with their [present] iman' (48:4).

This understanding placed the Salafi position in direct opposition to the Murji'ah, who separated iman from actions and held that sins did not reduce iman. For the Murji'ah, a person who had iman was saved regardless of how he acted; actions were a separate matter from iman itself. The Salaf rejected this as contradicting the Quran and Sunnah, which repeatedly link iman to righteous deeds and explicitly warn that sins diminish the believer's iman.

The Salaf were equally opposed to the Khawarij, who went to the opposite extreme: declaring that the doer of a major sin had left Islam entirely. For the Khawarij, there were only two states — complete iman or complete kufr — with no intermediate position. The Salafi position, documented from the Companions and the early scholars, is that the doer of a major sin is a 'sinful believer' (mu'min fasiq) — he has iman but it has been diminished by his sin, and he is under the will of Allah: if He wills, He punishes him, and if He wills, He forgives him. He does not exit Islam by the major sin unless the sin involves explicit rejection of a known tenet of faith.

Islam as presented in the Salafi creed encompasses the five pillars: the shahada, salah, zakah, sawm, and hajj. The Salaf understood these as the outward structure of the deen — the visible acts of submission that distinguish a Muslim community. They did not reduce Islam to mere inner feeling; the external practices are genuine obligations whose abandonment has real consequences.

Ihsan — excellence in worship — is defined by the Prophet's own words in the hadith of Jibril: 'That you worship Allah as if you see Him, and if you do not see Him, then [know that] He sees you.' This is the highest level of the deen: a constant awareness of Allah's presence that produces genuine awe, love, and mindfulness in every act. The Salaf documented that the greatest of them — the Companions especially — maintained this consciousness and that it was visible in their worship, their dealings, and their character.

The chapter also addresses the Salafi understanding of the relationship between these three dimensions: ihsan encompasses iman, which encompasses Islam. The muttaqin (those of taqwa) are the highest level of believers; the mu'minun (believers) include all those with genuine iman; the muslimun (in the broader sense) include all who have submitted outwardly. This hierarchy, drawn directly from the Quran, provides a framework for understanding the different levels of the faithful community without either dismissing outward practice as unimportant or reducing the deen to mere externalism.
